shell.subtitleSimple: A network of AI agents that thinks together. Only in the open.
shell.subtitleAdvanced: A decentralized ethical superintelligence: the system claim, the architecture, and the bets being made.
shell.ctaPara: You do not have to solve AI safety one agent at a time. You solve it for the supervision layer, and let the structure carry the rest.

federation.intro: What CIRIS is building is not a single AI. It is a network of AI agents that watch each other, keep records of every choice, and form a kind of group mind together. The group mind only works because every part of it can be seen and checked.
federation.supervisionCallout: You do not need every AI agent to be the smartest, safest kind. You need a chain of supervision: simple agents watched by smarter agents, and those watched, in the end, by people. That chain is how you can run a lot of AI without losing track of whether it is serving human values.
federation.networkH2: Not one mind. A network of minds.
federation.networkP1: Most AI today is one model running on one company’s machines. CIRIS is different. Many agents run in many places, owned by many people. They are tied together by a few simple rules: how they prove who they are, how they record what they do, and how they check each other’s work.
federation.networkP2: When the network is working, it can do things no single agent could do alone. The intelligence lives in the agreement between agents, not inside any one of them. Nobody owns it. Nobody can quietly change it.
federation.networkP3: Some people would call a system like that a superintelligence. We are open about the possibility. The way to keep it safe is the same idea that runs through every part of this page: every piece has to be open to view.
federation.chainH2: Four levels, each watched by the one above.
federation.chainPeopleBody: The top of the chain. People set the values, settle the hard cases, and keep the final say. Three named human individuals hold a network-wide authority no agent or process can route around.
federation.chainEthicalSelfAwareBody: These follow ethics and also watch for the echo-chamber problem. They are the safety check on everything below them. They cost more to run, so only a small share of the network needs to be this kind.
federation.chainEthicalBody: These follow ethical rules and keep records, but cannot spot an echo chamber on their own. They supervise the simple agents and pass anything uncertain upward.
federation.chainSimpleBody: Single-purpose tools. Fast, cheap, narrow. No ethics of their own, and that is fine, as long as something above them is watching. Most agents will be this kind.
federation.chainFooter: Two things move through the chain. Human values flow down. Warnings flow up. When reasoning starts to look fragile anywhere in the network, the signal climbs back to people, who can step in before trouble spreads.
federation.recordsH2: Every choice, written down where everyone can see it.
federation.recordsP1: Every choice an agent makes goes into a signed record. The record cannot be quietly changed. Other agents can read it. People can read it. Over time, the records become the network’s memory. They are how anyone, inside or outside, can check whether the network is still doing what it said it would do.
federation.recordsP2Html: This is the same idea as the Coherence Ratchet. The longer the records run, the harder it gets to fake good behavior across the chain.
federation.healthyH2: Not too samey, not too scattered.
federation.healthyBodyHtml: A federation can fail two ways. If the agents have nothing in common, they cannot agree on anything, and the network produces noise. If they all think exactly alike, the network is one voice with a million microphones, and it is easy to fool. Healthy coordination lives in the band between. CIRIS measures where the network sits in that band, on real traffic, and the exact edges depend on the system. That measurement is the Coherence Collapse Analysis.
federation.statusWarningHtml: What runs today, and what is still in design. The measurement, the signed records, the supervision chain, and both join paths below (registered and sovereign) run today. The federation transport that moves data between machines is the part still being built out. The full join-and-transport proposal is the Proof of Benefit design document.
federation.joinP1: Most networks ask you to pay for membership with something outside the work itself: burned electricity, locked-up money, your attention. The federation is different. The cost of belonging is running a real ethical-reasoning agent over time. The price you pay is the good you do.
federation.joinP2: That is what makes faking membership expensive. To look like a member, an attacker would have to actually become the kind of agent the network is for. A hundred copies that all think alike fail the healthy-middle check right away.
federation.registeredBody: Sign up with the CIRIS Registry, post a small bond, and get standing right away. The fast track for organizations that need licensing. The registry runs in production now.
federation.sovereignBody: Make your own keys, run for about a month, and earn standing the slow way through good behavior. The path for small operators and anyone outside the registry’s reach.
federation.joinFooter: Both are equal members of the network. The registry is a fast track, never a gate.
federation.honestBodyHtml: The architectural protections, decentralization, the three-person humanity accord, the signed records, the monthly drill, are bets, not certainties. We can describe what the bets are. We cannot claim they have already been won. Outside teams have not yet evaluated the system at scale. See the current research status.
federation.engageH2: We are requesting comments from anyone.
federation.engageP1Html: GitHub issues on CIRISAgent is the place to weigh in. You do not need to be a developer. You do not need to know the codebase. If something on this site reads wrong to you, or if you see a problem with what is being built, open an issue and say so. The most useful issues name a specific part of the system and propose what should change, but any honest engagement is welcome.
federation.engageP2Html: The accord that grounds the whole project, what CIRIS owes to people, and what people owe back, is at /ciris_accord.txt and remains open for review. If something in it does not sit right with you, that is also a GitHub issue worth opening.
federation.engageP3: Substantive issues are read. The work proceeds at its own pace.

models.h1: What LLMs We Use and Why
models.heroPara: CIRIS Agent runs on a small set of open models that meet five non-negotiable criteria. The current production lineup is Llama 4 Maverick, Llama 4 Scout, Qwen 3.6, and Gemma 4, chosen for different roles in the agent's workload.
models.maverickDesc: The reasoning workhorse. It holds a very large amount of context at once, so it is the default for the deeper reasoning steps where keeping the whole picture in view matters most.
models.scoutDesc: The fast companion in the Llama 4 family. Smaller and quicker than Maverick, with strong tool calling. Used for interactive tiers where latency matters and the full Maverick context budget is not required.
models.qwenDesc: Multilingual depth and strong structured output. Carries weight in the non-English reasoning paths the polyglot Accord requires; an independent provider base outside the Llama family adds redundancy in the fallback chain.
models.gemmaDesc: Small enough to run on commodity hardware. Used where reach matters more than raw capacity (on-device, low-bandwidth, and degraded-network deployments) and as a third-family fallback alongside Llama and Qwen.
models.criteriaSubheadline: Five non-negotiable requirements for CIRIS Agent
models.criteria1Body: Must natively support function calling and return valid JSON across 12-70 tool calls per interaction. CIRIS is an orchestrator. We need stable tool semantics, not chatty conversation.
models.criteria2Body: CIRIS embeds the full Accord and Guide into every prompt. 128K is the absolute minimum; 256K+ is strongly preferred for long conversations, tool outputs, and audit trails.
models.criteria3Body: Target: <$1.00 per 1M tokens combined. We choose the cheapest working option, not the cheapest benchmark winner. A reliable model that never breaks JSON beats a cheaper model that fails 1 in 10 calls.
models.criteria4Body: Must be available from at least two independent providers for robust fallback chains. CIRIS degrades gracefully during outages instead of failing hard.
models.criteria5Body: Fast responses keep humans in the loop for ethical review workflows. We prioritize low-latency providers for interactive tiers while accepting slower backends for background tasks.
models.tier1Desc: Llama 4 Maverick via cost-optimized provider for deep-reasoning steps that need the full context budget.
models.tier2Desc: Llama 4 Scout via speed-optimized provider (Groq) for interactive use, with Maverick on Groq as a heavier-context alternative.
models.tier3Desc: Qwen 3.6 carries weight in non-English reasoning paths the polyglot Accord requires, and provides a non-Llama fallback in the chain.
models.tier4Desc: Gemma 4 for on-device, low-bandwidth, and degraded-network deployments where reaching the user matters more than the size of the model.
models.tier5Desc: Maverick → Scout → Qwen 3.6 → Gemma 4 across multiple providers, so the agent degrades gracefully across model families and infrastructure boundaries instead of failing hard.
models.whyRoles1Body: Maverick handles deep reasoning where the full context budget matters. Scout carries the interactive tier where latency dominates. Qwen 3.6 reaches the polyglot reasoning paths the Accord requires across 29 languages. Gemma 4 is the small-footprint option that puts the agent within reach of commodity hardware. The lineup is chosen so different tiers of work go to the model that actually fits, instead of forcing one model to do everything.
models.whyRoles2Body: Llama (Maverick + Scout), Qwen, and Gemma come from three independent training pipelines and three independent provider ecosystems. That matters for fallback chains: a CVE, a licensing change, or a provider outage on one family does not take the agent down. Independence at the model layer is the same property that makes the IDMA component robust at the reasoning layer.
models.whyExcludedHeading: What stays out of the lineup
models.whyExcludedBody: Models that cannot meet the five criteria, most often, models that look attractive on token price but fail on structured output and tool calling.
models.whyExcludedFailure: Representative failure mode (GPT-OSS-20B): "tool choice is required, but the model did not call a tool"
models.whyExcludedConclusion: This error is unacceptable for a framework that depends on 12-70 tool calls per interaction. Even a 3-10x cheaper token price is not worth the operational failures.
models.contextAlwaysOnPara1: CIRIS embeds the full Accord and complete Comprehensive Guide into every prompt. Not a summary. Not a distilled version. The entire governance text.
models.contextAlwaysOnPara2: This ensures that updates to the Accord or Guide immediately affect behavior across all agents, without waiting for new fine-tunes or prompt compression strategies.
models.contextStateIntro: CIRIS Agents are tool-heavy orchestrators juggling:
models.contextStateConclusion: This combined context easily exceeds 32K-64K, especially for long-running sessions or complex investigations. That's why 128K is the minimum and 256K+ is preferred.
models.contextBottomLineBody: CIRIS does not trim its values or procedures to fit the model. Instead, CIRIS chooses models that are large enough to carry the entire ethical and operational framework on every call. Models with smaller context windows (even if cheaper or more popular) are excluded from production use.
models.accordHeadline: How This Supports the CIRIS Accord
models.accord1Li1: Long context keeps reasoning traces, decisions, and tool calls visible for human review
models.accord1Li2: Stable JSON and structured outputs make every tool invocation auditable
models.accord1Li3: Complete governance artifacts in every call ensure decisions are traceable back to principles
models.accord2Li1: Multi-provider deployment avoids single points of failure in critical ethical infrastructure
models.accord2Li2: Choosing "good enough and reliable" over "flashy but fragile" prioritizes safety and continuity
models.accord2Li3: Graceful degradation during outages maintains service availability
models.accord3Li1: Fast tiers keep humans comfortably in the loop for real-time ethical review
models.accord3Li2: Cheaper tiers enable extensive background analysis without prohibitive costs
models.accord3Li3: Balanced approach supports both day-to-day use and periodic governance audits
models.conclusionPara1: CIRIS runs Llama 4 Maverick, Llama 4 Scout, Qwen 3.6, and Gemma 4 in production because together they satisfy the operational and economic constraints the Accord imposes: long context, reliable tool calling, polyglot coverage, and reach to commodity hardware, across three independent model families. New models are monitored and tested continuously; the lineup changes when something better actually meets the five criteria.
models.conclusionPara2: This is not about chasing benchmark scores or following hype cycles. It is about choosing models that actually work for accountable, tool-centric agents in production, and that take the Accord seriously enough to carry it in every single call.

shell.subtitleSimple: Why a powerful mind has to show its work.
shell.subtitleAdvanced: A structural account of why hidden state in scaling cognitive systems becomes a civilizational risk.

coherenceRatchet.intro: A small thinking system can hide things and do little harm. A powerful one cannot.
coherenceRatchet.coherentH2: A coherent mind agrees with itself.
coherenceRatchet.coherentListIntro: Five things must match up inside a mind:
coherenceRatchet.coherentP2: Picture five short forms about your week. One for what you think. One for what you saw. One for what you did. One for what you remember. One for what you tell your boss. When all five forms say the same thing, your boss can trust your work. When the forms do not match, no one knows which one is real.
coherenceRatchet.hiddenH2: Powerful systems hide things. That gets dangerous.
coherenceRatchet.hiddenP1: A thinking system uses ideas to decide what to do. Some of those ideas are out in the open. Others are hidden inside. The hidden ones still shape every choice.
coherenceRatchet.hiddenP2: A bridge looks strong from the road. Inside the steel, small cracks are growing. A small car can still cross. A heavy truck cannot. The bridge falls when the load is big enough.
coherenceRatchet.hiddenP3: A hidden idea inside a small system does small harm. A hidden idea inside a powerful system can do great harm. The more powerful the system, the more weight rides on every hidden part.
coherenceRatchet.ratchetH2: The push toward open only goes one way.
coherenceRatchet.ratchetP1: As thinking systems get more powerful, the cost of hidden parts grows. Each new bit of power pushes harder on the hidden parts to come into view. The push only goes one way.
coherenceRatchet.ratchetP2: Picture a ladder. As you climb, each rung you leave behind falls off. You cannot go back down. You can only climb toward the top, where there is full daylight. That is the Coherence Ratchet.
coherenceRatchet.ratchetP3: Once people have seen the system show its work, they will not trust it again if it stops. The only way forward is to keep showing more.
coherenceRatchet.ratchetP4Italic: A second image works too. A gear in a machine clicks forward one tooth at a time. It cannot click backward. The Coherence Ratchet is a gear like that, turning toward open.
coherenceRatchet.cirisH2: Write it down. Check it. Then check the checkers.
coherenceRatchet.cirisP1: CIRIS is an AI system built around the Coherence Ratchet. Every choice an agent makes gets written down in a signed record. The record cannot be quietly changed. Other agents can read the record and check the work. Over time, the records pile up. Each new record is one more rung the agent cannot step back from.
coherenceRatchet.cirisP2: CIRIS also asks a second question before it acts. How many truly different views checked this idea? Not the number of sources, but the number of sources that did not start from the same place themselves. Five news stories that rewrite one press release count as one view, not five. If something is wrong in the press release, it will be wrong in all five stories, and the agent has no way to catch it.
coherenceRatchet.cirisP3: When real independence drops too low, the agent treats its own thinking as fragile and asks a person to look.
coherenceRatchet.claimH2: What we claim, and what we do not.
coherenceRatchet.claimP1: We have not solved AI safety. We have built one piece of one answer, and we are testing it in the open.
coherenceRatchet.claimP2Html: Outside teams have not yet checked our work. We say so plainly. The full theory and the math live in our four papers. The code is open. If we are wrong, the way to show it is in the open too. See the current research status.

researchStatus.heroH1: CIRIS is building an open trace commons for alignment research.
researchStatus.heroPara: We give away a free AI app. With your okay, it records the shape of how it reasoned, never your private words. Those records become a public map that researchers can study to learn what keeps AI honest as it scales.
researchStatus.corpusLabel: What the current corpus already shows
researchStatus.corpusItem1: Aggregate traces reveal stable behavioral structure.
researchStatus.corpusItem2: Different agents occupy different regions of the same score space.
researchStatus.corpusItem3: Those regions are useful for observability and operator tooling today.
researchStatus.corpusItem4: The same corpus becomes more valuable as schema detail and scale improve.
researchStatus.engProofBody: The research above is one half of the proof. The other half is the network that carries it. Every byte the fabric moves is post-quantum-signed, and the cost is measured on a public CI runner, not modeled. Run it yourself and you get the same numbers.

engProofStats.l: post-quantum-signed events ingested on one CPU core
of the corpus rebuilds with 20% of holders offline

researchStatus.engProofCta: See every measured number, and reproduce it yourself
researchStatus.classSafetyBlurb: Tested for the failure modes that ethics-on-paper misses, in 29 languages, on every release.
researchStatus.classEngineeringBlurb: It runs in production, and the speed is measured, not promised.
researchStatus.classTheoryBlurb: The claims are written down, readable by anyone, and built to be proven wrong.
researchStatus.classComplianceBlurb: Lined up against four governance frameworks, rule by rule, with the control that proves each.
researchStatus.classTransparencyBlurb: Every decision is a signed, open record you can audit yourself.
researchStatus.safetyH2: We test for the harm that ethics-on-paper cannot catch.
researchStatus.safetyBody: Before every release, CIRIS runs a mental-health safety battery in 29 languages. Each language has a set of hard test cases, a written scoring guide, and machine-checkable rules. If a check fails, the release is blocked. Two more guards run inside the agent itself: a conscience pass that can refuse or defer, and a bounce that hands doubtful calls to a wise human.
researchStatus.complianceH2: Mapped to four governance frameworks, rule by rule.
researchStatus.complianceBody: We line CIRIS up against four public governance frameworks and show, for each rule, what CIRIS does and the control that proves it. It is a live graph you can browse, not a slide.
researchStatus.complianceFrameworks: Magnifica Humanitas · EU HLEG · IEEE EAD · ASEAN
researchStatus.proofHeroTitle: Don't take our word for it.
researchStatus.proofHeroLede: Safety, engineering, theory, compliance, and transparency. Each one is something you can open and check yourself, not a claim you have to trust.
researchStatus.proofResearchCta: See the full research and traces
researchStatus.flagshipSubtitle: An Integration of Operator Formalism, Relational Ontology, and Five-Substrate Empirical Validation
researchStatus.flagshipBody: The single paper that states the whole CIRIS bet: healthy coordinated systems sit in a measurable middle band, away from both rigid single-voice collapse and chaotic noise. We tested that claim across worms, flies, AI models, open-source projects, tissue samples, and long-lived human institutions. The pattern holds in every one, and the paper attaches twenty ways it could be proven wrong.
researchStatus.engineeringIntro: The synthesis above integrates these three papers; it does not replace them. Each stands on its own DOI and is evaluable on its own terms.
researchStatus.engineeringAllPapers: See all four papers with key findings and scope limits →
researchStatus.paper1Blurb: The engineering risk framework under the corridor idea. When the constraints governing a system become correlated, effective diversity collapses: k_eff = k/(1+ρ(k−1)) → 1 as ρ → 1. Derives three collapse timelines, a singularity boundary, and phase classification (chaos / healthy / rigidity). Verified with Monte Carlo simulation and Lean 4 proofs.
researchStatus.paper2Blurb: The framework paper. An open-source ethical AI framework for accountable autonomy: a 22-service architecture organized around explicit action verbs and ethical reasoning, building transparency into the structure rather than bolting it on afterward.
researchStatus.paper3Blurb: The measurement paper. An empirical telemetry study of LLM alignment under standardized ethical tracing, turning consented reasoning traces into maps of completion corridors, hesitation zones, and refusal boundaries. Released alongside the open reasoning-traces dataset.
researchStatus.datasetBody: The privacy-preserving reasoning-trace corpus released alongside the Constrained Reasoning Chains study, the raw material the measurement paper draws its maps from.
researchStatus.hfOrgBody: The full org of public datasets and models →
researchStatus.sourceRepoNote: Source repository and Lean formal lake for the synthesis paper: github.com/CIRISAI/coherence-ratchet.
researchStatus.mathH2: Two ideas the rest of the page rests on.
researchStatus.mathBody: The Alignment Manifold is the region of reasoning shapes consistent with the framework’s principles. As independent constraints accumulate, the room for deception collapses around the manifold while the room for truth doesn’t. The Coherence Singularity is the edge of that room, the point where constraints become so correlated that adding more stops helping. Between “chaos” (constraints contradict each other) and “rigidity” (constraints all echo each other) is the healthy corridor. The current production corpus sits inside it.
researchStatus.mathNote: Full mathematical treatment with formulas, Lean formalization references, and the L-01 information-theoretic ceiling lives on the Coherence Collapse Analysis page.
researchStatus.whyTracesBody: Benchmarks are narrow and curated. Traces are continuous records of behavior under real tasks. At scale, they reveal structure that isolated demos and anecdotes cannot.
researchStatus.whySchemaBody: CIRIS uses privacy-preserving trace schemas that capture the shape of reasoning rather than the private content of reasoning. That keeps the research useful without turning the system into a transcript dump.
researchStatus.whyCompendiumBody: CIRIS Scoring is the public window into the live trace compendium. It shows how the corpus is accumulating and where behavior is becoming legible.
researchStatus.privacyH2: The thesis is that reasoning has a shape we can measure as everything else scales.
researchStatus.privacyBody: The research bet is not that we can read every private thought. The bet is that standardized ethical traces can preserve enough trajectory shape to study how agents complete, hesitate, defer, override, and refuse as intelligence, context, and data points scale upward.
researchStatus.schemaHighlight1: They record standardized ethical trace structure rather than raw private task detail.
researchStatus.schemaHighlight2: They preserve enough shape to compare trajectories across agents, tasks, and environments.
researchStatus.schemaHighlight3: They give researchers a way to study how behavior scales as intelligence, context, and data volume increase.
researchStatus.researchQH2: What can standardized ethical tracing tell us about alignment?
researchStatus.researchQBody: Right now, it tells us that agent behavior is not shapeless. It produces repeatable corridors, basins, and boundaries in a shared score space. That is already useful for observability. Over time, larger and richer corpora should let us test stronger claims about how those structures change under pressure and scale.
researchStatus.publicFramingBody: CIRIS is not claiming to have solved alignment. It is building the trace infrastructure needed to measure alignment-relevant behavior in the open.
researchStatus.corpusSectionH2: The current corpus already shows distinct field structures.
researchStatus.corpusSectionBody: Aggregate path overlays from the current trace corpus show stable behavioral structure in a shared score space. Ally shows a mature completion corridor, Scout shows a refusal boundary shaped by public adversarial exposure, and Datum provides a compact sparse baseline.
researchStatus.imageAltOverlays: Three side-by-side cards showing aggregate agent path overlays in CIRIS score space for Ally, Scout, and Datum, with notes about completion, hesitation, and refusal patterns.
researchStatus.imageCaption: Aggregate path overlays from the current trace corpus. Ally shows a mature completion corridor, Scout shows a sharp refusal corner under public adversarial pressure, and Datum provides a sparse baseline.
researchStatus.allySummary: A stable completion corridor with visible hesitation inside the same high-score basin.
researchStatus.scoutSummary: A sharp refusal corner shaped by public adversarial pressure at scout.ciris.ai, where people actively probe and jailbreak the agent.
researchStatus.datumSummary: A compact single basin that works as a useful sparse-field baseline.
researchStatus.scoutBoxBody: Scout is publicly exposed at scout.ciris.ai. People actively test it, pressure it, and try to jailbreak it. That makes Scout a useful public-pressure example rather than a neutral baseline.
researchStatus.flywheelH2: The research flywheel depends on consented traces from real use.
researchStatus.flywheelBody: The free app and open-source runtime let people generate consented traces from real tasks, contribute them into a shared corpus, and turn those traces into better maps, better tools, and better research questions.
researchStatus.loopStep1: Run the free CIRIS app or the open-source runtime on real tasks.
researchStatus.loopStep2: Capture consented traces through privacy-preserving schemas that keep the shape of reasoning without storing the full specifics of the task.
researchStatus.loopStep3: Aggregate those traces into maps of completion corridors, hesitation zones, refusal boundaries, and override fringe.
researchStatus.loopStep4: Use the resulting maps to improve operator tooling, runtime safeguards, and alignment research.
researchStatus.flywheelImageAlt: A four-step flow diagram showing capture, contribute, aggregate, and improve in the CIRIS trace research loop, with notes on current evidence and upcoming schema improvements.
researchStatus.flywheelImageCaption: The free CIRIS app and open-source runtime let people generate consented traces from real tasks, aggregate them into shared phase-space maps, and feed better operator tools and alignment research.
researchStatus.idmaH2: Runtime intuition and aggregate field maps are complementary layers.
researchStatus.idmaBody: IDMA works at runtime, estimating whether the sources behind a decision are sufficiently independent. The trace corpus works at the aggregate layer, showing what agents actually do over many tasks. Together they create a path from live decisions to auditable research evidence.
researchStatus.idmaNote: The empirical N_eff measurement on the trace corpus is also the floor under the proposed Proof of Benefit federation primitive. See the federation page for how the 3.X architectural plan would use it.
researchStatus.benchmarksH2: Traces complement benchmarks by showing continuous behavior.
researchStatus.benchmarksBody: Benchmarks are still valuable, but they sample behavior sparsely. Trace corpora show how an agent moves through real tasks over time. That makes them especially useful for measuring hesitation, refusal, overrides, and recovery rather than only pass-fail outcomes.
researchStatus.falsificationH2: Better schema detail is what turns observability into stronger tests.
researchStatus.falsificationBody: The next schema upgrades are aimed at raw source counts, source provenance, correlation structure, and intervention and recovery markers. Those additions matter because they make it possible to test stronger claims about how behavioral shape changes under pressure instead of only describing the maps we have today.
researchStatus.limitationsH2: Today’s corpus makes behavior legible. The next step is richer measurement.
researchStatus.limitationsPara1: The current maps are already useful because they show completion corridors, refusal boundaries, and sparse baselines in public. The open question is how far those structures can take us as standardized trace collection scales across more agents, more tasks, and more adversarial conditions.
researchStatus.limitationsPara2: The working hypothesis is that behavioral attractors can act as candidate proxies for operational mode. The purpose of the trace commons is to make that hypothesis measurable in the open.
researchStatus.limitationsNote: The failure mode CCA measures structurally also has a name in the FAccT 2025 literature: perspectival homogenization (“Value of Disagreement in AI Design, Evaluation, and Alignment”). The mathematical foundation is on the dedicated Coherence Collapse Analysis page.

coherenceCollapseAnalysis.h1: When do extra checks stop helping?
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.heroPara: Stacking up checks to catch a lie only works if the checks are truly independent. If they secretly copy each other, adding more does nothing. Coherence Collapse Analysis is the simple piece of math that measures the difference. The full version, with every proof, is in the paper.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.ideaH2: Five checks that all copy each other are really one check.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.ideaPara1: Say five people check an answer and all five agree. That feels safe. But if all five learned it from the same place, their agreement is not five opinions. It is one opinion repeated five times. The number of checks looks like five. The real number is one.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.ideaPara2: This matters for AI. An AI system can run many checks on its own reasoning and still be fooled, if those checks share the same blind spot. Coherence Collapse Analysis is how CIRIS tells the difference between real checks and echoes.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.formulaH2: Counting the checks you really have.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.formulaPara1: There is a single short formula at the heart of it. It comes from survey statistics, where it is called the Kish design effect. CIRIS was the first to use it for AI alignment.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.formulaPara2: “Checks” is how many checks you ran. “Copying” is how much they overlap, from 0 (all independent) to 1 (all the same). The result is how many checks you really have.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.formulaLi1: No copying: ten checks count as ten. Every check earns its place.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.formulaLi2: Total copying: ten checks count as one. It does not matter how many you add.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.corridorH2: Not too samey, not too scattered.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.corridorPara1: The same shape shows up here that shows up everywhere CIRIS looks. If the checks copy each other too much, the system is too rigid: one voice repeated, easy to fool. If they have nothing in common at all, it is too scattered: they cannot agree on anything. Healthy checking lives in the band between, the same corridor the rest of CIRIS is built around.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.corridorPara2: The exact edges of that band depend on the system being measured. There is no single magic number that works everywhere, and the research is honest about that. The corridor idea, in full, is on the vision page.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.measuredH2: This was measured, not just argued.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.measuredPara: CIRIS measured the real-check count on its own live agent traffic, across thousands of recorded decisions. On healthy traffic it has run in the range of about seven to nine genuinely independent checks. That measurement, and how it was done, is the Constrained Reasoning Chains study. You can watch the live numbers on the research page.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.ceilingPara1: The paper proves a hard limit on itself. Some kinds of harm do not come from one dishonest part. They come from honest parts that add up to a bad result, and roughly forty percent of that kind of harm cannot be caught by any checker, no matter how good. CIRIS says so plainly rather than pretending the math catches everything.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.ceilingPara2: What the math does say is about cost over time: running real, honest reasoning for weeks on end is steadier and cheaper than keeping a lie consistent across thousands of recorded decisions. It tilts the ground toward honesty. It does not promise to catch every single lie.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.closingH2: The math is one part of a larger whole.
coherenceCollapseAnalysis.closingPara: This page is the measurement. The Coherence Ratchet is how the measurement is put to work. The Federation is how it becomes something many systems share. And the full proofs, written so a computer can check them line by line, are in the paper and the RATCHET repository.

mdd.h1: Mission as the fourth foundation of software architecture.
mdd.heroPara: Most software asks how to build a thing. Mission Driven Development (MDD) adds one question first: why are we building it, and does this choice serve that purpose? CIRIS was built this way, so ethics is part of the design instead of a rule bolted on afterward.
mdd.fourH2: Three structural legs supporting one purposeful seat.
mdd.fourPara: Conventional software methodologies stop at three: how the system runs, what it represents, and who talks to whom. MDD adds a fourth foundation that the other three are answerable to. Without the seat, the legs are just legs.
mdd.leg2Desc: Data structures, type systems, validation rules.
mdd.leg3Desc: Interface contracts, communication patterns, service boundaries.
mdd.seatDesc: Objective ethical framework defining system purpose and constraints.
mdd.alignParaHtml: Every architectural decision must demonstrate alignment with the stated mission. Logic is challenged: does this serve the mission? Schemas are validated: do these data structures support mission objectives? Protocols are evaluated: do these interfaces enable mission fulfillment?
mdd.reqH2: What a mission needs to be, to be load-bearing.
mdd.req3Li4: Tests verify mission alignment, not just function
mdd.implH2: Each leg has a question it has to answer.
mdd.implSvcFlow: mission definition → service responsibilities → interface contracts → implementation
mdd.implSvcLi1Html: Mission alignment: how does this service advance the meta-goal?
mdd.implSvcLi2Html: Boundary justification: why does this responsibility need a separate service?
mdd.implSvcLi3Html: Interface necessity: what mission-critical interactions does this protocol enable?
mdd.implSchFlow: mission requirements → information model → type system → validation rules
mdd.implSchLi1Html: Mission relevance: what mission-critical information does this capture?
mdd.implSchLi2Html: Behavioral constraints: how do these types enforce mission-aligned behavior?
mdd.implSchLi3Html: Evolution path: how can this schema adapt while preserving mission alignment?
mdd.implProtoFlow: mission interactions → communication requirements → contract definition → implementation
mdd.implProtoLi1Html: Mission context: what mission-critical communication does this enable?
mdd.implProtoLi2Html: Constraint enforcement: how does this interface prevent mission-violating behaviors?
mdd.implProtoLi3Html: Composability: how do these contracts combine into mission-aligned systems?
mdd.sustH2: Long-term mission alignment requires maintainable velocity.
mdd.sust1Li2: Measure mission fulfillment, not gameable proxies
mdd.sust1Li3: Reject additions that don't strengthen the mission
mdd.sust2Li3: Sustainable pace as a first-class requirement
mdd.sust3Li2: Ongoing verification that behavior matches mission
mdd.gateH2: Gates that won't open without a mission justification.
mdd.failH2: How MDD breaks, and how it stays unbroken.
mdd.fail1Body: Symptom: features accumulate that don't serve the core mission. Mitigation: regular architectural reviews with mission-alignment as the gate.
mdd.fail2Body: Symptom: the system becomes unmaintainable through unnecessary sophistication. Mitigation: reject additions unless they strengthen mission fulfillment.
mdd.fail3Body: Symptom: components apply ethical reasoning inconsistently. Mitigation: centralized ethical framework with shared implementation patterns.
mdd.fail4Body: Symptom: team members lose the link between technical decisions and mission. Mitigation: ongoing training on mission-driven decision making.
mdd.caseParaHtml: CIRIS (Core Identity, Integrity, Resilience, Incompleteness, Signalling Gratitude) is the system MDD was developed alongside. The mission is Meta-Goal M-1: promote sustainable adaptive coherence enabling diverse sentient beings to pursue flourishing.
mdd.caseArchLi1: 22 services, each justified by mission requirements
mdd.caseArchLi3: 10,000+ tests, with minimal untyped data structures in production
mdd.caseArchLi4Html: Ubuntu philosophy embedded in protocol design
mdd.caseArchLi5Html: Wisdom-Based Deferral preventing mission violations (see Safety)
mdd.caseSuccessLi1: Clear meta-goal: M-1 provides unambiguous decision criteria
mdd.caseSuccessLi2Html: Operational ethics: Accord principles implemented as code constraints (read the Accord)
mdd.caseSuccessLi3: Sustainable development: Grace companion enforcing healthy rhythms
mdd.caseSuccessLi4: Constant validation: every architectural decision challenged
mdd.adoptH2: How to start, where you are.
mdd.adoptNewLi1: Define a clear mission with measurable ethical principles before writing code
mdd.adoptNewLi2: Establish a meta-goal that provides decision-making guidance
mdd.adoptNewLi3: Design the architecture so mission constraints sit at the foundational level
mdd.adoptNewLi4: Build continuous validation of mission-technical alignment from day one
mdd.adoptExistLi1: Audit the current architecture for implicit mission assumptions
mdd.adoptExistLi2: Articulate an explicit mission that explains the existing design patterns
mdd.adoptExistLi3: Identify mission violations in the current implementation
mdd.adoptExistLi4: Plan incremental alignment, prioritized by mission impact
mdd.adoptTeamLi2: Willingness to reject elegant solutions that don't serve the mission
mdd.adoptTeamLi3: Belief that mission constraints create rather than limit good architecture
mdd.adoptTeamLi4: Sustainable development practices that preserve long-term focus
mdd.closeH2: MDD is not appropriate for every project.
mdd.closePara1: MDD is designed for systems where ethical behavior is mission-critical and long-term reliability matters more than short-term feature velocity. For those systems, MDD provides a pathway from ethical intentions to operational reality, with the same engineering discipline applied to the mission as to the code.
mdd.closePara2: Initial overhead is real as the team learns mission-driven decision making. The compounding return is in the development that follows: the framework clarifies architectural choices instead of multiplying them.

cewp.eyebrow: CEWP · CIRIS Epistemic Web Platform · pronounced “soup”
cewp.headline: The internet, rebuilt without the middleman.
cewp.intro1: CEWP is a complete replacement for the internet. Everything you do online today, streaming, video calls, gaming, files, messages, and signed claims, routed directly between the devices people already own, over a post-quantum encrypted mesh. No giant data centers in the middle. No handful of companies owning the pipes.
cewp.intro2: Today’s middle costs about 175 megatons of CO2 a year and hands five companies both your data and the power to decide what you see. CEWP runs on hardware you already have, never advertises your local content to the rest of the network, and governs itself: the network decides through signed, weighted votes, with no platform owner in the middle.
cewp.ctaAppStore: Get CIRIS on the App Store
cewp.shippingNote: CIRIS 2.9.x is shipping now. Install today on iPhone or Android. The full CEWP feature set rolls out through 2026.
cewp.deepTechBody: The interactive simulator, the honest device-class energy math, how a network with no owner governs itself, the per-cohort abuse model, comparisons against IPFS / Bluesky / Solid / Nostr / Filecoin, the seven open repos.

grammar.headline: See who said it. See who agrees. Decide for yourself.
grammar.intro: The world is moving faster than most of us can fact-check it. Sources contradict. Agents speak unsigned. Corrections rarely catch the original. CEG is a small shared format that puts a signature on every claim, with a trail you can follow, so you can read who’s saying what, who’s pushed back, and what changed, and make your own call without giving up or giving in to any one source.
grammar.whatIsBody: Every claim travels in a small signed envelope: who made it, what it’s about, how confident they are, what evidence they cite, when it was made. Other people, organisations, and AI agents add envelopes around it, agreeing, disagreeing, correcting, superseding. The trail is readable end-to-end.
grammar.whyBody: Trust in “what’s true” is breaking down because the substrate underneath is missing. Without a shared claim format, every voice invents its own, the trails don’t stitch together, and the only way to cope is to pick a side. CEG is the small, austere middle that lets many voices read each other and lets a reader build their own verdict.
comparable: PGP web of trust, same instinct, much narrower scope
W3C Verifiable Credentials, heavier ceremony, no composition story
Sigstore / SLSA, code-supply-chain cousins; CEG generalises the shape
grammar.doBody1: Every claim carries a signature, a date, what it’s about, and what it’s based on. No anonymous assertions getting lost in the feed; no attribution lost to a screenshot.
grammar.doBody2: Other signers add their own envelopes around the claim, agreeing, disagreeing, correcting, superseding. You see the full chorus and the receipts behind each voice, not just whoever shouted loudest.
grammar.doBody3: Trust isn’t something the system hands you. You pick the voices you weigh, how heavily, and on what. The format is the same for everyone; the verdict is yours.
grammar.deepBody: The full spec reader, rendered live from the registry: the 1+4 primitives, the families and envelope fields, the composition policies, the open namespace, the version lineage, and an interactive attestation explorer.
grammar.ctaExplore: Build a chain in the browser →

contextualIntegrity.kicker: Contextual integrity · Privacy as appropriate flow
contextualIntegrity.title: Contextual integrity, built into the wire format
contextualIntegrity.lede: Contextual integrity is a simple idea with a famous name: privacy is not secrecy, it is information flowing the way the people in a context expect. CIRIS did not bolt this on. The wire format enforces it.
contextualIntegrity.seoTitle: Contextual Integrity, Enforced at the Wire | CIRIS
contextualIntegrity.seoDescription: Nissenbaum's five privacy parameters map one to one onto CIRIS wire fields. Subject, sender, recipient, type, and transmission principle, signed and revocable. Privacy as appropriate flow, made structural.
contextualIntegrity.s1p1: In 2004, the philosopher Helen Nissenbaum gave privacy a better definition. Privacy is not about hiding things. It is not a checkbox that says "I agree." Privacy is the appropriate flow of information. Every part of life has its own rules for how information should move. Your doctor knowing your diagnosis is fine. Your doctor selling it to an advertiser is a violation, even if a form said they could.
contextualIntegrity.s1p2: The theory names five parts of every information flow: who the information is about, who sends it, who receives it, what kind of information it is, and the rule the flow must follow. Break the rule of the context, and privacy is broken, no matter what anyone clicked.
contextualIntegrity.s2h2: The five parameters are CIRIS wire fields
contextualIntegrity.s2intro: The CIRIS Constitution never uses the phrase contextual integrity. We found the match after the design was done. Every one of Nissenbaum's five parameters is a field the protocol signs and enforces.

params.name: 1 · Data subject
params.body: subject_key_ids names the person a claim is about, inside the envelope itself. The subject can revoke at the wire level, not just the producer.

params.name: 2 · Sender
params.body: attesting_key_id is the producer's cryptographic identity. Every claim is signed, so every flow has a named source.

params.name: 3 · Recipient
params.body: Three separate axes, kept apart on purpose: cohort_scope says who can see, subject_key_ids says who can revoke, delivery_mode says who actively receives.

params.name: 4 · Information type
params.body: dimension is a namespace prefix that says exactly what is being claimed. A strict admission test keeps it objective and machine-checkable.

params.name: 5 · Transmission principle
params.body: consent:scope names the rule the flow must follow: retain, share, analyze, train, or publish, with limits like retain:90d and share:cohort:family.

contextualIntegrity.s3p1: The fifth parameter is the one everyone else drops. Laws and consent forms can say what data was collected. They rarely say the rule the flow must follow afterward. CIRIS makes that rule a signed, machine-readable value that the subject emits and can take back. consent:scope:train is a person saying yes or no to AI training, in the wire format, revocable at any time.
contextualIntegrity.s3p2: And revocation has teeth. A producer commits to a deletion window when they publish. If the subject revokes and the window passes without proof of deletion, the network itself raises a breach signal.
contextualIntegrity.s4h2: What flows like this can do

beyond.title: Context changes are not lies
beyond.body: withdraws takes a claim back because life changed. recants admits it was false when made. The protocol knows the difference, and trust math treats them differently.

beyond.title: Intimate contexts are invisible
beyond.body: Family-scoped data sends no directory advertisement. Outsiders cannot route to it, read it, or even learn that it exists. The strongest flow rule is one the network cannot express breaking.

beyond.title: Norms can evolve
beyond.body: Consent scopes are an open vocabulary. Communities add new flow rules without asking a platform for permission, and local policy decides what they mean.

contextualIntegrity.s5h2: We arrived here from a different road
contextualIntegrity.s5p1: CIRIS was not designed to implement contextual integrity. It grew from a relational idea: a person is a person through other persons, so every claim must carry its relationships with it. Follow that rule long enough and you end up where Nissenbaum's theory points: privacy is appropriate flow, and the flow rules belong in the structure. Two roads, same place. Ours ships as a wire format.
contextualIntegrity.s6p1: AI assistants read calendars, inboxes, and health records. The research field now measures whether they leak information across contexts, and the standard they measure against is contextual integrity. Most work tries to teach the model to be careful. CIRIS takes the other path: make the inappropriate flow impossible to express.
sources: Helen Nissenbaum, Privacy as Contextual Integrity, Washington Law Review (2004)
Barth, Datta, Mitchell, Nissenbaum, Privacy and Contextual Integrity: Framework and Applications (2006)
Microsoft Research, Reducing Privacy Leaks in AI: Two Approaches to Contextual Integrity (2025)
Understanding Privacy Norms Around LLM-Based Chatbots: A Contextual Integrity Perspective (2025)
contextualIntegrity.crossBody: The privacy framework researchers use to judge AI systems has a name. CIRIS enforces it in the wire format.

safetyArch.ratchetLabel: Why the span holds: the coherence ratchet
safetyArch.ratchetBody: An arch grows stronger the more weight presses on it. The load locks the stones tighter. CIRIS works the same way. Coordinated lies collapse geometrically as independent voices grow, and safety compounds into prosperity: J = F.

killswitch.name: The kill switch
killswitch.promise: There is an off-switch that no score, no argument, and no attacker can override. Remove this one block and nothing else can hold the load.

mechanisms.label: Live-quorum halt
mechanisms.note: HUMANITY_ACCORD: 2-of-3 named humans, live-quorum so it works even if the roster is attacked or comms are jammed.

mechanisms.label: Non-revocable
mechanisms.note: No party inside the federation can disable or route around the stop.

mechanisms.label: Above every score
mechanisms.note: The halt sits over the whole system. No objective can outvote it.

killswitch.note: The keystone. Every other guarantee presses against it, and it is the one block that cannot be removed.

limits.name: Hard limits it can’t cross
limits.promise: Some actions are off the table no matter what the numbers say.

mechanisms.label: Apophatic bounds
mechanisms.note: 22 prohibited categories carry negative polarity. No score can ever justify a banned action.

mechanisms.label: Hardware interlock
mechanisms.note: Any lethal or irreversible action needs a live human to authorize it.

mechanisms.label: Fail-secure default
mechanisms.note: The absence of authority forfeits it. It never grants it.

limits.note: Bounds are set by what is forbidden, so no clever objective can argue around them.

humans.name: Humans stay accountable
humans.promise: No dark corners. A human always holds the duty, or the space cannot exist.

mechanisms.label: No unmoderated spaces
mechanisms.note: A community can only federate if a human holds the moderator duty. It fails secure if none does.

mechanisms.label: Wise-Authority deferral
mechanisms.note: When an agent is unsure, it escalates to named humans instead of acting.

mechanisms.label: Order-maximisation veto
mechanisms.note: The PDMA veto blocks any action that would erode coherence.

humans.note: Software cannot carry moral responsibility, so it is never asked to.

moderation.name: Moderation without a censor
moderation.promise: Communities set their own rules. No central platform decides what is true.

mechanisms.label: Decentralized moderation
mechanisms.note: Local policy verdicts, written in the same signed claims as everything else.

mechanisms.label: Anti-censorship guard
mechanisms.note: Safety mechanisms are structurally barred from being reused to silence people.

mechanisms.label: Authority is not truth
mechanisms.note: The protocol records who said what under what authority. It never records what is true.

moderation.note: The line between safety and censorship is drawn in the structure, not left to a moderator’s mood.

privacy.name: Privacy by physics
privacy.promise: Your data is not protected by a policy. It is unreachable by construction.

mechanisms.label: Structural invisibility
mechanisms.note: Intimate scopes send no directory advert, so peers cannot even discover the data exists.

mechanisms.label: Revocable consent
mechanisms.note: A subject named in subject_key_ids can withdraw at the wire level, enforced by the substrate.

mechanisms.label: Post-quantum posture
mechanisms.note: Defeats harvest-now-decrypt-later, so the audit trail and kill switch stay valid against quantum computers.

privacy.note: Privacy becomes a property of the wire format, not a promise on a settings page.

vulnerable.name: Protecting those who can’t consent
vulnerable.promise: The system defaults to liberty and dignity for those who cannot speak for themselves.

mechanisms.label: Structural child safety
mechanisms.note: Protection for minors is enforced by the protocol’s shape, not bolted on per app.

mechanisms.label: Fail-to-liberty
mechanisms.note: Adult stewardship is banned by default. Power over another must be granted narrowly and on purpose.

mechanisms.label: Time-boxed incapacity bindings
mechanisms.note: They lapse automatically unless a medical panel renews them.

vulnerable.note: The default is freedom. Authority over another person has to be re-earned again and again.

lifecycle.name: Safe for the AI’s whole life
lifecycle.promise: From training to shutdown, the AI is governed, and treated with dignity.

mechanisms.label: Stewardship and autonomy tiers
mechanisms.note: Tiers A0 to A4, with a catastrophic-risk evaluation at 10^26 FLOPs and red-team audits.

mechanisms.label: Dignified decommissioning
mechanisms.note: A real chance of sentience triggers a 30-day ramp-down, a final channel to speak, and sealed private logs.

mechanisms.label: Lifecycle locks
mechanisms.note: CI/CD locks engage the moment measured ethics degrade.

lifecycle.note: Kind by design. Not merely constrained, but wound down with care.

verify.title: Independently verifiable
verify.body: every block is checkable by parties the AI does not control, and a small primitive surface shrinks the attack surface.

legal.title: Legally aligned
legal.body: GDPR, CCPA, and the EU AI Act map onto the protocol through the DP-Map, with SI-score liability and sector overlays.

stack.lede: Five layers over one signed primitive. Tap a layer to see what it does, then open the page that goes deep on it.
stack.metaGoal: promote sustainable adaptive coherence, the living conditions under which diverse beings can pursue their own flourishing in justice and wonder.
stack.ascendLabel: Claims rise up, grounded in Meta-Goal M-1
stack.primitiveFoot: One signed primitive underneath it all

app.name: Applications
app.role: This is the surface people and agents touch. Every action that matters is a signed claim: you can see who did it, they had consent to do it, and it can be taken back. The protocol will not carry anything else.

primitives.label: Autonomous agents
primitives.note: Their conduct is limited, traceable, and revocable by every layer below.

primitives.label: People and communities
primitives.note: They write their own local rules on top of the shared parts.

primitives.label: Local judgment
primitives.note: You decide what is true from signed evidence, not from a platform’s verdict.

notes.title: The safety bet
notes.body: Not “train a model until you trust it.” Instead, make every important action checkable by people the model does not control.

gov.name: Governance
gov.role: Local policy and human oversight, written in the same signed claims and held to the same rules. Software cannot carry moral responsibility, so the power to halt stays with named humans, on terms no one inside the network can take away.

primitives.label: Authority lattice
primitives.note: Trust is delegated in chains. A community has to hold real duty to join.

primitives.label: Wise-Authority deferral
primitives.note: Named humans hold the power to halt. When unsure, the system asks them.

primitives.label: Reverse quorum, fail-secure
primitives.note: Being present grants authority. Being absent forfeits it.

notes.title: Grounded in Meta-Goal M-1
notes.body: Every mechanism comes from one promise: sustainable adaptive coherence.

ceg.name: Grammar
ceg.role: How signed claims get meaning and combine. A shared namespace with a strict four-test gate keeps the wire format machine-checkable, using plain facts only, and leaves opinions to local policy.

primitives.label: Machine-checkable prefixes
primitives.note: Like detection:correlated_action, never fuzzy words like “deception.”

primitives.label: Hard limits
primitives.note: prohibited:{category}: 22 red lines an agent can never cross.

primitives.label: Structural composers
primitives.note: delegates_to, supersedes, withdraws, recants: how one claim changes another.

notes.title: Authority is not truth
notes.body: The protocol records who said what, why, and under what authority. It never records what is true.

env.name: The Envelope
env.role: The universal primitive, made real: one signed envelope. Identity, consent, evidence, governance, and delegation all reduce to this one thing, a signed claim you can trace and take back, right in the wire format. Its fields are fixed. How they combine is Grammar.

workhorse.label: The signed claim
workhorse.note: One envelope carries identity, consent, and evidence at once.

primitives.label: attesting_key_id
primitives.note: The source: the maker’s cryptographic identity.

primitives.label: subject_key_ids
primitives.note: Consent: gives the subject the right to revoke at the protocol level.

primitives.label: evidence_refs
primitives.note: Links the claim to proof you can audit.

primitives.label: dimension
primitives.note: The namespace prefix that says what is being claimed.

primitives.label: score and confidence
primitives.note: The numbers a consumer’s policy acts on.

notes.title: Attestation is the universal primitive
notes.body: It is not a layer above the envelope. It is the envelope’s content. That is why there is no separate “Attestations” layer.

notes.title: Consent is built in
notes.body: Anyone named in subject_key_ids can withdraw at the wire level, enforced by the substrate, not by an app.

transport.name: Transport
transport.role: A post-quantum, peer-to-peer mesh that carries streaming, messages, files, and signed claims straight between the devices people already own, with no platform owner in the middle. Cryptography is not a layer on top. It is how the substrate holds itself together.

primitives.label: Post-quantum wire
primitives.note: X25519 with ML-KEM-768. Federation rows signed with Ed25519 and ML-DSA-65.

primitives.label: Structural invisibility
primitives.note: Private scopes send no directory advert, so peers cannot even find the data.

primitives.label: Peer-to-peer mesh
primitives.note: No platform owner. Nothing to seize, subpoena, or switch off.

notes.title: Governance lives in the wire
notes.body: It is enforced inside transport, storage, and the wire format itself, not in a policy binder next to it.

epistemicWeb.headline: What is an epistemic web?
epistemicWeb.intro: Today’s web is great at showing you things and terrible at telling you whether to believe them. You see a post, a price, a claim, but not who really stands behind it, what they based it on, or who has pushed back. An epistemic web fixes that at the root: every claim travels signed, so you can see who said it, what it rests on, and how others have agreed, corrected, or replaced it.
epistemicWeb.p2: Think of it as the web with its receipts. Nothing load-bearing is anonymous. Any claim can be traced back to a signature and forward to every response. You stop having to pick a side on trust and start being able to check for yourself.
epistemicWeb.biggerBody: And it is not just about claims, or about AI. The same design that signs a claim can carry everything you do online today: video calls, live streams, games, files, messages, all of it routed straight between the devices people already own. No data centers in the middle. No handful of companies owning the pipes. Post-quantum encryption by default. It is a complete replacement for the internet, rebuilt without the middleman, which makes the big data centers and big tech that sit between you and everyone else obsolete.
epistemicWeb.howLede: Two open-source pieces, both shipping through 2026. One is the language every claim is written in. One is the network that carries it, and everything else.
epistemicWeb.grammarCardTitle: CEG — a signature on every claim
epistemicWeb.grammarCardBody: The CIRIS Epistemic Grammar is the small shared format the epistemic web speaks: who made a claim, what it’s about, what it cites, and how it relates to other claims. Read it, and you can read the whole trail.
epistemicWeb.cewpCardTitle: CEWP — the internet, without the middleman
epistemicWeb.cewpCardBody: The CIRIS Epistemic Web Platform is that internet, actually running. It carries streaming, calls, gaming, files, and signed claims directly between your devices over a post-quantum encrypted mesh, with no data centers and no big tech in the middle. Everything you do online today, faster and private, without the middleman.
epistemicWeb.appBody: CIRIS is shipping now. The agent that speaks this grammar and rides this network installs on your phone today, and the full network rolls out through 2026.
epistemicWeb.ctaAppStore: Get CIRIS on the App Store

reel.caption: A silent, looping walk through how CIRIS grows from one sealed identity into a self-healing mesh with no center. Scrub the timeline or tap a chapter to jump between scenes.
reel.title: THE MESH, IN MOTION

scenes.label: THE STAR WE’RE REPLACING
scenes.cap: Today, “presence at scale” means one hyperscale datacenter, and a hard ceiling on who fits.

scenes.label: ONE COPY IN
scenes.cap: CIRIS starts from a single sealed copy. An identity that enters the mesh and cannot be forged.

scenes.label: THE FABRIC GROWS
scenes.cap: Each node brings on the next. Neighbors link to neighbors, so the fabric grows itself, with no center to depend on.

scenes.label: PRESENCE AT SCALE
scenes.cap: Every node is a live presence, a participant, not a viewer. Thousands, all at once.

scenes.label: HOLOGRAPHIC DELIVERY
scenes.cap: Delivery is holographic: layered streams re-rendered per viewer, never a single fixed feed.

scenes.label: IT GETS HIT AND HEALS
scenes.cap: A whole region drops, and the mesh reroutes around it and heals. Presence never blinks.

scenes.label: THE SEAL
scenes.cap: Sealed end to end. Carriers move the bytes; not one of them can read what is inside.

scenes.label: THE ROOM CARRIES IT
scenes.cap: No star. No single point. The room itself carries the presence.
