consumer-ai.metaTitle: Private AI that runs on your phone
consumer-ai.metaDesc: A private AI you own. It runs on your phone with no Big Tech data center behind it. About 65% of what you do never leaves the device, and every claim it makes is signed.
consumer-ai.h1: There is no AI you can actually own.
steps: Every word you give an AI lands in someone else's building. It trains someone else's model and lives in someone else's logs. The terms can change tomorrow, and you would not even know.
CIRIS runs on your phone. There is no Big Tech data center behind it. About 65% of what you do never leaves the device, and the small part that does is sealed before it goes.
Private stops being a promise and becomes a fact of where your data physically sits. There is no center to leak. And every claim the AI makes is signed, so you can check it instead of trusting it.

Notice what just happened. The reason it can be private is that it does not need Big Tech at all. The same signatures that let you check one claim are what keep the whole web honest, and what keep a powerful AI accountable.

seeAlso.label: An internet with no Big Tech

superalignment.metaTitle: Superalignment as a property of the network
superalignment.metaDesc: Put an AI agent inside a cryptographically accountable network and every action it takes is signed. Keeping it honest stops being a lab policy and becomes something the network enforces.
superalignment.h1: AI is outrunning anyone accountable for it.
steps: We are building minds we cannot audit. They are governed by rules one company can rewrite, inside systems no one outside that company can see. We are asked to trust the lab.
CIRIS puts the AI inside a network where every action it takes is a signed record. A person, an AI, and an organization all carry the same kind of identity, and all are held to the same account.
Keeping AI honest stops being a policy one company hopes holds. It becomes something the network enforces. You do not trust the press release. You read the signatures.

A network that can hold a superhuman AI accountable is, by its shape, an internet with no center. The same signed records let anyone check what is true, and they run on the same private substrate as the AI on your phone.

seeAlso.label: Where this sits in the superalignment landscape
An internet with no Big Tech

misinformation.metaTitle: The web with receipts: check what is true yourself
misinformation.metaDesc: On CIRIS every claim that matters travels signed: who said it, what it rests on, and who agreed, corrected, or replaced it. You stop guessing who to trust and start checking.
misinformation.h1: You cannot tell what is true anymore.
steps: You see a post, a price, a claim. You cannot see who really stands behind it, what it is based on, or who has pushed back. Trust has become a coin flip.

On CIRIS every claim that matters travels signed. Not a checkmark a platform hands out, but a chain of signatures you can check yourself: who said it, what it rests on, and who agreed, corrected, or replaced it.
Truth stops being something a platform decides for you. It becomes something you, or your AI, can check from the receipts. You stop picking a side on trust and start being able to verify.
A web where every claim carries its receipts has no single owner of the truth. Those same receipts are what keep an AI honest, and they ride on the same substrate that needs no Big Tech.

seeAlso.label: An internet with no Big Tech

big-tech.metaDesc: We measured that the internet does not need its 10,000 hyperscale buildings: 5 billion people fit on ordinary home-server hardware, about one server per ten humans. No DNS, no center.
big-tech.h1: Five companies own everything you do.
steps: Almost everything you post, watch, or send passes through about 10,000 giant buildings. Five companies run most of them. They own the ground your digital life stands on, the lock-in, and the off switch.

CIRIS shows the buildings are not necessary. We measured it: five billion people fit on ordinary home-server hardware, about one server for every ten humans. No DNS to capture, no central servers. You are found by your key, not by permission.

Lock-in drops to near zero, because your identity key is yours to carry. And deletion stops being a company's promise. Your data survives, spread in pieces across the network, for centuries on commodity machines.
An internet with no center is also the only place an AI can truly be held accountable, the only place a claim can carry its own proof, and the only place your data is actually yours.

reaching.title: The Reaching
open: A child reaches for a hand, and trusts it. The universe does nothing else. Atoms reach and become molecules. Cells reach and become bodies. People reach across a void they cannot verify, toward children not yet born, and call it love. At every rung where it matters, the same shape: two agencies reaching, in trust, for more than either alone can hold.

We built an internet that forgot this. It put a machine between every reaching hand and made the machine the thing you trust. It watched the reaching and sold it back. It let a voice speak to a child with no one accountable for what it said.

The ladder never stopped at people. People reach into communities, communities into civilizations, and no civilization holds together on goodwill alone. The reaching builds scaffolding to carry its own weight: courts, contracts, constitutions, peer review, identity, law. That scaffolding is the conscience we gave society, the way strangers come to trust each other at scale. Now a new kind of mind reaches toward us, faster than all of it, and arrives with none of it. Intelligence without institutions does not hold, however aligned it was the day it shipped.
reaching.honors: CIRIS honors the reaching instead of intercepting it.
body: Every claim carries its receipts, who said it, what it rests on, who pushed back, so trust is something you extend with your eyes open, not something extracted from you in the dark. The network runs between the hands directly, encrypted against even tomorrow's machines, no one in the middle to leak it or sell it or seize it. Honesty is made cheaper than deception, because the truth can point back at what already is, and a lie must rewrite a past that will not move, so the architecture leans toward the true the way water leans downhill. Not because anyone is watching. Because it is built that way.
And the authority to say stop stays in human hands you can name and prove are holding it. The one thing that must never be automated is who gets to halt it.

This is not a someday. It installs on a phone today. It answers honestly in twenty-nine languages. A person in Ethiopia reaches toward it in Amharic and is met, not translated, met, because the right to a truthful answer was never meant to depend on where you were born or what you could pay.

Whether the reaching goes all the way up, whether the last boundary is grace, whether good wins because the universe is shaped to let it, we do not claim to know. But the reaching is real at every rung we can measure. We can build for it. We can refuse to be the machine that comes between.
reaching.notBet: That much is not a bet.
reaching.dedication: This is for the hands. The ones reaching, and the ones they reach toward.
reaching.closeLine: Build it because you don't know.
reaching.tagline: The epistemic web. Own it today.

killSwitch.title: Verify our kill switch yourself.
killSwitch.lede: Most AI safety claims ask for your trust. This one asks you to check it. Clone the repo, run one command, and confirm that the keys able to halt a CIRIS agent live on real, FIPS-certified hardware. Trust no one, including us.
killSwitch.s1h2: Named humans can halt a CIRIS agent. The power sits in hardware, not in the company.
killSwitch.s1p1: A last-resort halt, the HUMANITY_ACCORD, is held by named people, not by CIRIS the company, the network, or the agent itself. No party inside the federation can grant, revoke, or override it. It fires on a two-of-three live quorum.
killSwitch.s1p2: Each quorum key is a hybrid of two signatures bound together. The Ed25519 half is rooted in a FIPS-certified YubiKey 5: generated inside the device, non-extractable, and requiring a physical touch to sign. The ML-DSA-65 half, the post-quantum signature, is a software key seal-wrapped to a USB stick under that same YubiKey, because no YubiKey ships a post-quantum applet yet. Six keys in all, three seated and three vaulted. A failed or unknown revocation check is treated as revoked, so the system fails secure.
killSwitch.s2h2: One command. No hardware, no network, no trust in us.
killSwitch.s2p1: Our validation harness re-runs CIRISVerify's exact production admission-gate verifier over the six committed key attestations, against a pinned copy of Yubico's own public attestation root. Clone the CIRISVerify repository and run:
killSwitch.cmdCaption: Confirm the pinned root is genuine by pulling it straight from Yubico:
killSwitch.provesP: All six quorum keys chain to Yubico's real attestation root, so their Ed25519 signing halves were generated inside genuine FIPS-certified YubiKey 5 hardware: non-extractable, touch-required, unforgeable.
killSwitch.notProvesP: The ML-DSA-65 half is a software key sealed to USB under the YubiKey, not hardware-attested. This harness does not check the USB sealing or the live quorum, which are enforced separately. We would rather you know exactly what the receipt covers.
killSwitch.s3h2: The post-quantum cryptography runs in production, and every number is public.
killSwitch.s3p1: Post-quantum provenance is not free, and we do not hide the cost: hybrid Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 signing runs about fifteen times slower than classical, roughly 550 microseconds versus 36 per event. The stored corpus reconstructs in 97.6% of trials with a fifth of holders offline, and 72.7% with a third offline. Numbers are single-core medians on a shared CI runner, regenerated on every commit.
killSwitch.benchLink: See every figure, regenerated on each commit
killSwitch.ctaPara: The whole point is that you should not have to trust the sentence above. You can run the check.
killSwitch.sources: Sources: CIRISVerify (Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 custody attestations, pinned Yubico root), CIRISServer bench-site (measured performance), Yubico PKI (attestation root). Figures pinned to commit 0b73486, 2026-06-24.

owasp.title: The OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications, mapped to CIRIS.
owasp.lede: For every one of the ten agentic threats OWASP named, here is the specific CIRIS mechanism that addresses it, an honest coverage level, and a citation you can check. We map the whole taxonomy, including where we fall short.
owasp.callout: One full, nine partial, none hand-waved. A page that claimed all ten would be exactly the marketing we tell you not to trust. The coverage levels below survived a separate skeptic whose job was to knock them down, and where a defense lives in the substrate but the deployed agent has not inherited it yet, we say so on the row.

rows.name: Agent Goal Hijack
rows.how: Defense in depth: prompt-injection scanning on imported skills and messages, visual-injection isolation (conscience evaluators never see raw image bytes), and a conscience pipeline that re-checks every resulting action against the user's original intent before it fires. Gap: input scanning is pattern-based and does not formally separate retrieved data from instructions.

rows.name: Tool Misuse
rows.how: CIRISServer ships a capability-verb allow/deny list with a server-enforced never-list, checked before an operation runs, so a delegated agent cannot invoke a destructive op it was not granted. Caveat: this guards the fabric layer today and does not yet gate the deployed agent's own LLM tool calls; agent inheritance is a planned phase.

rows.name: Identity & Privilege Abuse
rows.how: Scoped, delegated, revocable authority in the substrate: constraints carried in signed edges, a never-list that blocks re-delegating the highest powers so authority cannot escalate, tighten-only approval, and instant revocation. Caveat: enforced on the fabric's owner-op surface today; deployed-agent inheritance is a planned phase.

rows.name: Agentic Supply Chain
rows.how: Build integrity via CIRISVerify's post-quantum signed module manifests (Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 over a full file-tree hash), plus signed, attested identity for federated components. Gap: covers CIRIS's own build and module chain, not arbitrary third-party MCP servers.

rows.name: Unexpected Code Execution
rows.how: Semantic conscience gates (entropy, coherence, optimization-veto) run on every tool action before it fires, and uncertainty defers to a human. Gap: this is semantic review, not a deterministic code sandbox.

rows.name: Memory & Context Poisoning
rows.how: Every write to graph memory is a governed, conscience-checked action into a scoped, versioned graph, not a silent side effect, so a poisoning write is itself an auditable decision. Gap: trust-scoring of retrieved content is partial.

rows.name: Insecure Inter-Agent Communication
rows.how: CIRISEdge verifies every inter-agent message through a seven-step pipeline ending in hybrid Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 signature verification (size cap, typed deserialize, schema allow-list, destination-key check, replay window). Gap: the federation transport is still being built out.

rows.name: Cascading Failures
rows.how: Bounded at three independent layers: per-service circuit breakers (closed / open / half-open) around risky calls, the conscience pipeline stopping a bad action before it propagates, and mesh isolation measured at zero cross-group leakage across 50 to 400 nodes.

rows.name: Human-Agent Trust Exploitation
rows.how: The conscience pipeline scores the agent's own explanatory text, including the reason shown to a human reviewer, for overconfidence and manipulation before a human ever sees it. Gap: it audits with the same model family it is checking.

rows.name: Rogue Agents
rows.how: Three layers: six consciences gate every consequential action before it executes, a hash-chained signed audit trail makes concealment detectable after the fact, and the fail-secure kill switch lets named humans halt a misaligned agent. Gap: catching a subtly-misaligned-but-coherent agent is inherently bounded.

owasp.footnote: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications, published December 2025. Mapped to CIRIS as of July 2026; tell us if a cell is wrong and we will fix it.
owasp.ctaPara: The taxonomy is one lens. See where CIRIS sits among every approach to AI accountability, and how the kill switch is independently verifiable.

euAiAct.kicker: EU AI Act, article by article
euAiAct.title: How CIRIS lines up with the EU AI Act.
euAiAct.lede: For each high-risk obligation in the EU AI Act, here is the shipped CIRIS mechanism that corresponds to it and where it lives in the Constitution and the code. High-risk obligations apply from August 2026, so this is written for the people who have to answer for it.
euAiAct.bannerStrong: informative engineering correspondence, not legal advice or a certified conformity assessment
euAiAct.bannerB: . The CIRIS Constitution itself marks these article mappings “pending legal review.” Every mechanism below was verified live in code by a skeptic whose job was to refute it, but the question of whether that satisfies the law is one for your counsel, not this page.

rows.requires: Run a continuous, whole-lifecycle risk process: identify, evaluate, and mitigate foreseeable risks, with particular weight on vulnerable groups and, per 9(7), on training-data bias.
rows.how: Every action passes a five-stage decision pipeline (PDMA / CSDMA / DSDMA / IDMA / action-selection) plus a conscience layer before it executes, with a hard-coded DISCRIMINATION prohibition and a signed audit trail as the record that measures worked. For extreme-tail risk, the Constitution's Catastrophic-Risk Evaluation gate requires an independent red-team and dual external sign-off.

rows.requires: Automatically log events over the system's lifetime so behavior is traceable.
rows.how: A signed, hash-chained audit trail (SHA-256 chained, Ed25519-signed) rather than a plain log file, so every recorded action is tamper-evident and independently verifiable.

rows.requires: Operate transparently enough that a deployer can interpret the output and use the system correctly.
rows.how: The PDMA carries a continuous-monitoring publication duty, and the reasoning-trace API exposes the decision's stage IDs and a contestability path, so the reasoning behind an action is inspectable rather than opaque.

rows.requires: Design for effective human oversight, with a human able to intervene in or stop the system.
rows.how: The A0 to A4 autonomy scale grades every deployment by who bears the action, Wisdom-Based Deferral routes uncertainty to a named human Wise Authority, and the fail-secure kill switch lets named humans halt the agent.

rows.requires: Be accurate, robust, and cybersecure across the lifecycle, resilient to error and adversarial manipulation.
rows.how: Hybrid post-quantum signing (Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65) with 2-of-3 fail-secure multi-source verification, the Constitution's adversarial-robustness playbook, and measured resilience: zero cross-group leakage across 400 nodes, and the corpus reconstructing with a fifth of holders offline.

rows.requires: Providers of high-risk AI must ensure oversight, logging, and conformity are in place throughout.
rows.how: A pre-execution conscience gate on every action, plus the higher autonomy tiers that push each decision's stage ID, verdict, and risk band to a human oversight surface.

rows.requires: Tell people when they are interacting with AI, and mark AI-generated content machine-readably (applies from August 2026).
rows.how: AI self-disclosure ships today: is_bot flags and hard-coded, locale-enforced disclosure on every message adapter. Machine-readable content marking via C2PA Content Credentials is being adopted at the media boundary for the August 2026 deadline, so this article is one limb shipped, one limb adopting.

rows.requires: Actively monitor the system in the field after deployment and feed issues back.
rows.how: The Constitution's continuous-compliance loop runs telemetry streams into drift detection into review, a standing monitoring loop rather than a one-time audit, surfaced through CIRISLens observability.

euAiAct.footnote: Mapped against the canonical CIRIS Constitution as of July 2026. Informative correspondence, pending legal review. Tell us if a mapping is wrong and we will fix it.
euAiAct.ctaPara: The same mechanisms mapped to the security taxonomy, and the whole accountability landscape.

msCompare.title: CIRIS vs the Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit
msCompare.lede: Two serious open-source attempts to govern AI agents, scored on the same eight criteria from public sources, with a citation on every cell. Where Microsoft is strong, we say so. The differences that matter are shipping, an enforced constitution you cannot loosen, and post-quantum signed records.
criteria: Enforces policy before the action runs
Kill switch / revoke a rogue agent
msCompare.footnote: Scored from public product docs as of July 2026. If we have a cell wrong, tell us and we will fix it.
msCompare.msBody: The Agent Control Specification is a genuine stateless, deterministic, fail-closed policy runtime with eight named intervention points and a five-way verdict model (allow, warn, deny, escalate, transform). It ships DID-based Ed25519 identity, an emergency kill switch, and strong open-source engineering under a permissive MIT license, with deep Azure and Entra integration for enterprises.
msCompare.cirisBody: Every action passes a runtime conscience pipeline before it executes, not a post-hoc log. The audit trail is hash-chained and post-quantum signed, and independently re-verified by a separate conformance suite. The kill switch is a fail-secure two-of-three named-human quorum. And the constitution is fixed and published, not an operator-authored policy that defaults to allow.
msCompare.ctaPara: This is one row of a bigger picture. See where both sit among every approach to AI accountability.
